questions.prompt: What is the correct passive form of 'They build new houses every year'?

options.text: New houses are built every year.
New houses were built every year.
New houses have been built every year.
New houses are being built every year.

questions.explanation: The active sentence is in the present simple ('build'), so the passive uses 'is/are + past participle': 'are built'. Option B (past simple passive) would be for 'They built...'. Option C (present perfect passive) would be for 'They have built...'. Option D (present continuous passive) would be for 'They are building...'.

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence where the by-agent is BEST omitted.

options.text: The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci.
The package was delivered by the courier.
The new law was proposed by the president.
The play was written by Shakespeare.

questions.explanation: 'By the courier' is the most expendable by-agent: it is obvious that a delivery person delivers packages, so the agent adds no new information. In options A and D, the author (da Vinci / Shakespeare) is the crucial new information. In option C, the president as proposer is important political context.

questions.prompt: Which of the following sentences uses the passive voice INCORRECTLY?

options.text: The report was submitted on time.
The accident was happened near the school.
The film has been released in three countries.
The road is being repaired this week.

questions.explanation: 'The accident was happened' is incorrect because 'happen' is an intransitive verb — it has no object, so it cannot be passivised. The correct sentence is simply 'The accident happened near the school' (active). Options A, C, and D all correctly passivise transitive verbs (submit, release, repair).

questions.prompt: Which sentence is an example of the GET-passive?

options.text: The window was broken during the storm.
The decision has been made by the committee.
She got promoted last month.
The letter will be sent tomorrow.

questions.explanation: 'Got promoted' is the get-passive (get + past participle) — informal register, often used for unexpected or significant personal events. Options A and D use 'was/will be' (be-passive). Option B uses 'has been' (present perfect passive). The get-passive is only option C.

questions.prompt: Which passive transformation is CORRECT for: 'They are building a new hospital'?

options.text: A new hospital is built.
A new hospital was being built.
A new hospital is being built.
A new hospital has been built.

questions.explanation: The active is in the present continuous ('are building'), so the passive is 'is/are being + past participle': 'A new hospital is being built'. Option A (present simple passive) would correspond to 'They build'. Option B (past continuous passive) would be for 'They were building'. Option D (present perfect passive) would be for 'They have built'.
